The Interface是一个关于社交媒体和民主交集的每日专栏和时事通讯。点击此处订阅。
周二上午，马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)通过视频聊天与员工举行了一次会议，以解决公司决定不对特朗普总统最近发布的一些帖子采取行动的担忧。正如我周四报道的那样，这一决定在员工中引发了异常多的内部异议，周一它蔓延到公众面前，数百名员工实际上举行了罢工。
员工们发给我一份周二会议的录音，它提供了一个宝贵的窗口，让我了解马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)的决策进展，他计划的下一步行动，以及一些员工持续不断的不满。我的同事阿迪·罗伯逊和我一起收听。她写道：
虽然扎克伯格说他应该向员工提供更多的透明度，但他坚持对特朗普的帖子进行“相当彻底”的评估，称避免贴标签或删除这些帖子的选择是困难的，但却是正确的。
根据The Verge获得的一段录音，扎克伯格描述了对特朗普最近发布的帖子感到不安，其中一篇帖子警告抗议者，“当抢劫开始时，枪击就开始了。”但是“我知道我需要把我的个人意见分开.。扎克伯格说：“从我们的政策和我们运营的平台的原则来看，我们知道我们做出的决定会导致公司内部的很多人非常不安，我们会受到很多媒体的批评。”
在某种程度上，高层管理人员和罢工参与者之间的争端是棘手的-你要么认为特朗普的职位应该下台，要么不应该。但如果你假设在美国总统大选前还会有更多这样的决定要做出，就像我一样，了解Facebook是如何为其政策决定辩护和沟通的，这是很有价值的。上周，特朗普将该公司置于糟糕的境地-如果事件继续像过去几天那样展开，他很可能很快就会让它们陷入更糟糕的境地。今天的会议可以帮助我们了解当这种情况发生时，Facebook可能会做出什么反应。
如果内乱升级，Facebook可能会对美国的州政府行为者采取临时言论限制。扎克伯格说：“如果我们正在进入一个可能会有长期内乱的时期，那么这可能表明，我们需要不同的政策，即使只是暂时在美国呆了一段时间，与我们以前所处的地方相比也是如此。”“对于这种情况，我们有一些先例。”他说，该公司已经采取行动消除新冠肺炎的错误信息，因为它代表着公共卫生紧急情况，而且对使用国家武力的过度监管也同样可以被认为是紧急情况，需要做出政策改变。
扎克伯格承认与特朗普讨论了他的帖子，但表示电话是在他决定不发帖子后才打的。扎克伯格曾因在做出决定的当天亲自与一项高调决定的主题交谈而受到批评-这一特权授予Facebook上极少的内容审核主题，而且这一决定让我认识的一些员工感到失望。
只有一名黑人员工参与了特朗普的最终决定。这是Facebook的全球多样性官员玛克辛·威廉姆斯(Maxine Williams)说的。扎克伯格补充说，政策和诚信团队中有更多的黑人员工，但员工们追问他，为什么讨论中没有包括更多的黑人声音。
Facebook有一个七点计划，以解决员工对特朗普帖子决定的担忧。扎克伯格说，脸书将重新审查有关各州威胁使用武力的政策；重新审查可能让人们利用与新冠肺炎签约的威胁来压制投票的政策；考虑为不违反脸书社区标准但在某种程度上令人反感的内容增加新的标签；更好地在内部沟通政策决策的方式；在政策团队中纳入更多样化的观点；从员工那里征求新的倡议，以促进脸书上的种族正义；并以新冠肺炎信息中心为蓝本，创建一款帮助人们投票的新产品。
扎克伯格认为，Facebook保持特朗普的帖子可能会损害公众对该公司的看法。他说：“这一决定很可能给公司带来了巨大的实际成本，因为我们认为这是正确的一步。”
扎克伯格鼓励员工将捍卫言论自由视为一项崇高的事业。他指出，一名警察谋杀乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)的原始视频已经发布到Facebook上，引发了全球抗议。Facebook员工应该对此感到自豪，他说：“我会敦促人们不要仅仅从伤害和缓解的角度来看待我们所做的事情的道德影响。这显然是我们必须做的很大一部分-我不是在淡化这一点。..。但它也是关于好的一面，给了人们一个以前不能进入新闻和谈论东西的声音。这也是一个好的方面，给了人们一个以前不能进入新闻和谈论东西的声音。让痛苦的事情变得显而易见。我认为这也很重要。“。
特朗普有一条不能越过的红线，Facebook已经执行了这条红线。昨天，我在这里写道，员工的许多挫折感可以追溯到担心特朗普做不了什么来促使Facebook删除他的一个帖子。扎克伯格今天向员工指出，Facebook确实在3月份删除了特朗普的广告，这些广告误导用户认为竞选调查实际上是美国人口普查。这就是说，一般来说，删除广告比删除正常帖子的争议要小得多-据我所知，特朗普甚至从未对广告情况发表过评论。
扎克伯格担心言论自由只会逐渐减少，我们总有一天会后悔。他说：“随着时间的推移，一般来说，我们倾向于增加更多的政策，以限制越来越多的事情。”“如果每次有争议的事情，你的直觉是，好吧，让我们限制很多，那么你最终确实会限制很多我认为最终对每个人都有好处的事情。”
与我交谈过的员工似乎并不特别被这些答案打动。其中一人告诉我：“每个人都很感激我们有机会直接与他解决这些问题。”“与此同时，没有人认为他给出了一个真正的答案.”另一人说，扎克伯格在电话中看起来“真的很害怕”。“我想他害怕他的员工背叛他，”这名员工说。“至少这是我从面部表情和语气中得到的。”
与此同时，另一名员工告诉我，扎克伯格的决定得到了公司大多数人的支持，但同意的人不敢直言不讳，因为担心显得麻木不仁。(一名在电话会议上发言的员工呼应了这一点。)
我认为，你如何看待特朗普帖子引发的争议，最终取决于你认为明年会如何发展。一些人预测将恢复平衡；另一些人预计暴力言论将持续升级。另一种说法是，有乐观主义者和悲观主义者。
乐观主义者认为，社交媒体既不好也不坏，只是社会可以使用的一种强大的新工具。它的滥用比它的积极用法更受关注，但那些积极的用法-例如引起人们对警察谋杀无辜男子的注意-最终超过了消极的用法。乐观主义者说，今年可能是特别令人担忧的时刻，但最终(甚至可能很快！)，情况将开始恢复正常，我们将很高兴我们保留了言论自由的传统，这样像黑人生命也是(Black Lives Matter)这样的运动可以继续使用这些工具来实现积极的目标。在这个框架中，扎克伯格是一个乐观主义者。
悲观主义者认为，社交网络正在被坏人玩弄，他们利用社交网络来重新执行和加剧现有的权力失衡。例如，总统利用这些平台呼吁警察和军队“控制”和平抗议者，镇压受宪法保护的集会-利用他自己的言论自由，再加上社交平台提供的算法推广，以压制他人的言论自由。悲观主义者知道，你永远不可能真正让美国总统下台，但她宁愿不帮助一家有钱有势的公司建造扩音器。特别是考虑到她知道更糟糕的职位即将到来，而且集体损害可能只会增加。
乐观主义者相信，历史的漫长弧线是朝着正义的方向发展的。悲观主义者打开电视，看到法西斯士兵已经在地面上，向他们的美国同胞发射催泪瓦斯。乐观主义者可能有时间和特权等待任何内乱结束。悲观主义者可能不会。
⬆️趋势：Grindr正在从其应用程序中删除种族过滤器。这一决定是为了向美国针对警察对黑人男女施暴的抗议活动表示声援。这是伟大的一步-种族过滤器已经成为该平台上无数种族主义互动的来源。(Ashley Carman/The Verge)。
⬇️走下坡路：谷歌正在发布冠状病毒阴谋论的网站上投放广告，帮助他们的所有者创造收入并继续运营。至少在一个例子中，谷歌投放了一位已经被禁止的阴谋家的广告。(瑞安·加拉格尔(Ryan Gallagher)和马克·伯根(Mark Bergen/彭博社)。
非营利组织民主与技术中心(⭐)对唐纳德·特朗普提起诉讼。该组织表示，特朗普针对社交媒体公司的行政命令侵犯了这些公司的言论自由权利。但我以为总统喜欢言论自由！“纽约时报”的凯特·康格报道：
民主与技术中心(Center For Democratic And Technology)在一份法律文件中说，特朗普的命令“显然是报复性的”。“它攻击私人公司Twitter，因为它行使第一修正案对总统声明发表评论的权利。”
该组织认为，这一命令还可能阻止其他公司自由发言。文件称：“特朗普总统-通过公开攻击Twitter并发布命令-试图冷却其他演讲者未来的在线言论。”
推特限制了众议员马特·盖兹(R-FL)的一条推文，因为他违反了反对美化暴力的政策。在此之前，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)上周也采取了类似的行动。这条推文确实令人不安，我很高兴推特采取了行动。(Makena Kelly/The Verge)。
微弱多数的美国人认为推特将特朗普总统的一条推文标记为违反暴力规定是正确的。不出所料，根据美国国家研究集团(National Research Group)的一项调查，自由派更有可能支持Twitter，保守派更有可能反对这一决定。(Ina Fry/Axios)。
唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)对推特的愤怒吸引了人们对该平台的更多关注。这对Twitter的底线是有利的。(Sarah Frier/彭博社)。
关于乔治·弗洛伊德抗议活动的错误信息在推特上继续激增。阴谋论者正在散布谣言，说弗洛伊德还活着，乔治·索罗斯正在资助抗议活动。(Davey Alba/《纽约时报》)。
过去一周，唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)的暴力言论在全国街头的实际暴力中得以实现。我的同事TC Sottek认为，随着他和他的盟友现在威胁要进行军事镇压，是时候禁止他们在Facebook和Twitter上使用了。(The Verge)。
上周末，有关MSNBC播放Z世界大战镜头而不是真实抗议图片的错误信息在Twitter上疯传。一个人把它作为一个笑话发到了推特上，然后随着这篇帖子的疯传，他试图关闭它，但已经太晚了。(Adi Robertson/The Verge)。
一个冒充Antifa的白人民族主义组织在Twitter上呼吁暴力。“今晚我们说‘F-城市’，然后我们搬进居民区……。白色的引擎盖..。我们拿着属于我们的…“。他们在推特上发帖，试图在抗议活动中煽动暴力。(本·柯林斯，布兰迪·扎德罗兹尼和伊曼纽尔·萨利巴/NBC)
奥克兰大体上和平的抗议活动在最后演变成暴力事件，警方指控抗议者违反宵禁。在网上，冲突的图片占据了报道的主导地位。(Zoe Schiffer/The Verge)。
抗议的规模是包罗万象的，查理·沃泽尔写道。这是一种独一无二的集体创伤，主宰了整个国家的意识，没有其他渠道可以观看，也没有分心的事情。(查理·沃泽尔/《纽约时报》)。
澳大利亚一家法院裁定，在Facebook上发布自己文章的媒体机构对其他Facebook用户对这些帖子的诽谤言论负有责任。在最初诉讼中作为被告的媒体公司，包括新闻集团和悉尼先驱晨报，表示他们正在考虑向该国最高法院提出进一步上诉。(迈克·切尔尼/《华尔街日报》)。
特朗普政府表示，将对欧盟已经采用的数字商务税展开调查。此举升级了一场全球争夺战，将影响Facebook和亚马逊等科技巨头的纳税地点。(Jim Tankersley和Ana Swanson/《纽约时报》)。
随着用户公开支持“黑人生命也是命”运动，⭐TikTok正在成为一个政治平台。一年前，TikTok被指审查与香港抗议活动有关的视频。该公司当时表示，这些视频没有获得点击量，因为它们不是用户想看的。路透社的Arriana McLymore和Echo Wang报道：
截至周二上午，TikTok的热门话题#BlacklivesMatter已经以30亿的浏览量跃居榜首。像查利·达梅里奥(Charli D‘Amelio)这样的超级巨星，他的6000万粉丝几乎是HBO美国订户数量的两倍，他在展示舞蹈动作时暂停了一下，以讨论明尼苏达州黑人乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)，他作为一名白人警察跪在脖子上去世，引发了一场关于种族和权力的全国性辩论。
“我将继续传播这些信息，并成为一名盟友，”身为白人的达梅里奥在周末获得超过4770万次点击量和1200万个赞的帖子中说。
“断章取义”的推特账号正在变成抗议账号，以支持正在进行的反对警察暴行的运动。创建者列出了供人们捐赠的保释基金，并发布了关于安全抗议的建议。(朱莉娅·亚历山大/The Verge)
Spotify、苹果音乐、亚马逊音乐和YouTube音乐已经承诺通过特别的播放列表、默哀时刻和社交媒体封锁来支持周二的封锁。这场运动旨在抗议警察的暴力和种族主义，并向乔治·弗洛伊德致敬。(尼克·斯塔特(Nick Statt)和乔恩·波特(Jon Porter)/The Verge)。
周二的停电帖子使用了#BlackLivesMatter标签，无意中淹没了抗议活动。这是一个高风险的例子，说明了使用标签进行组织的局限性。(詹姆斯·文森特/The Verge)。
前Facebook高管巴里·施尼特(Barry Schnitt)在Medium上给员工写了一封公开信。“Facebook说，甚至可能相信，它是站在言论自由一边的。事实上，它已经把自己放在了利润和懦弱的一边，“他说。(巴里·施尼特/Medium)。
谷歌翻译仅支持大约100种语言。在大流行期间，当COVID PSA需要以准确和文化上合适的方式被翻译成尽可能多的语言时，这是一个问题。(Gretchen McCulloch/连线)。
本季度变焦收入增长了169%。该公司还将今年的收入指引翻了一番。(Jordan Novet/CNBC)。
捐款支持为正义而战。我保证这样做之后你会感觉更好；这个链接有很多想法。
请阅读这篇关于反种族主义的帖子。它是面向教育工作者的，但我从中学到了很多。
Zoom的首席执行官埃里克·袁(Eric袁)刚刚开始在Zoom上谈论他的公司的井喷式收益电话会议，他在pic.twitter.com/NV0VQ14SEQ上静音
-乔丹·诺维特(@jordAnweet)2020年6月2日。

note_en:
The Interface is a daily column and newsletter about the intersection of social media and democracy. Subscribe  here.
 On Tuesday morning, Mark Zuckerberg held a meeting with employees over video chat to address concerns related to the company’s decision not to take action on some recent posts by President Trump. As I reported on Thursday, the decision  sparked an unusual amount of internal dissent among employees, and on Monday it spilled over into public with  a virtual walkout of a few hundred employees.
 Employees sent me a recording of the Tuesday meeting, and it offers a valuable window into Mark Zuckerberg’s decision-making progress, his planned next steps, and the ongoing dissatisfaction of some employees. My colleague Adi Robertson listened in with me.  She wrote:
 While Zuckerberg said he should have offered more transparency to employees, he stood by what he called a “pretty thorough” evaluation of Trump’s posts, saying the choice to avoid labeling or removing them was difficult but correct.
 According to a recording obtained by  The Verge, Zuckerberg described being upset by Trump’s recent posts, one of which warned protesters that “when the looting starts, the shooting starts.” But “I knew that I needed to separate out my personal opinion ... from what our policy is and the principles of the platform we’re running are — knowing that the decision that we made was going to lead to a lot of people being very upset inside the company and a lot of the media criticism we’re going to get,” said Zuckerberg.
 On some level, the dispute between top executives and the walkout participants is intractable — you either think Trump’s post should have come down, or shouldn’t have. But if you assume there will be many more decisions like this to be made in the run-up to the US presidential election, as I do, it’s valuable to understand how Facebook justifies and communicates its policy decisions. Last week, Trump put the company in a bad position — and if events continue to unfold as they have for the past few days, he’ll likely soon put them into a worse one. Today’s meeting can help us understand how Facebook might react when that happens.
  Facebook might adopt temporary speech restrictions for state actors in the United States if civil unrest escalates. “If we were entering a period where there may be a prolonged period of civil unrest, then that might suggest that we need different policies, even if just temporarily in the United States for some period, compared to where we were before,” Zuckerberg said. “And we have some precedents for what that might look like.” He said that the company had moved to remove COVID-19 misinformation because it represented a public health emergency, and that excessive policing of the use of state force could similarly be considered an emergency that would warrant policy changes.
 Zuckerberg acknowledged talking to Trump about his posts, but said the phone call happened only after he had decided to leave them up. Zuckerberg had been criticized for personally talking to the subject of a high-profile decision on the day he made it — a privilege granted to exceedingly few subjects of content moderation on Facebook, and a decision that disappointed some employees I know.
 Only one black employee was involved in the final Trump decision. That was Maxine Williams, Facebook’s global diversity officer. Zuckerberg added that there are additional black employees on the policy and integrity teams, but employees pressed him on why there weren’t more black voices included in the discussion.
 Facebook has a seven-point plan to address employee concerns over the decision about Trump posts. Zuckerberg said Facebook will re-examine policies about states threatening the use of force; re-examine policies that could let people use the threat of contracting COVID-19 to suppress the vote; consider adding new labels for content that doesn’t violate Facebook’s community standards but is objectionable in some way; communicate better internally about how policy decisions are made; include more diverse viewpoints on the policy team; solicit new initiatives to advance racial justice on Facebook from employees; and create a new product modeled on the COVID-19 information hub that helps people vote.
 Zuckerberg thinks that Facebook keeping the Trump posts up likely damaged public perception of the company. “Likely this decision has incurred a massive practical cost for the company to do what we think is the right step,” he said.
 Zuckerberg encouraged employees to see defending free speech as a noble cause. He noted that the original video of George Floyd’s murder by a police officer had been posted to Facebook, sparking global protests. Facebook employees should take some measure of pride in that, he said: “I would urge people not to look at the moral impact of what we do just through the lens of harm and mitigation. That’s clearly a huge part of what we have to do — I’m not downplaying that. ... But it’s also about the upside, and the good, and giving people a voice who wouldn’t have previously been able to get into the news and talk about stuff. And having painful things be visible. I think that matters, too.”
 There   is  a red line Trump can’t cross, and Facebook already enforced it. Yesterday I wrote here that much employee frustration can be traced to concerns that there’s nothing Trump could do that would prompt Facebook to remove one of his posts. Zuckerberg noted for employees today that Facebook actually did remove Trump ads in March  that misled users into thinking that a campaign survey was actually the US Census. That said, it’s generally much less controversial to remove an ad than a regular post — so far as I can tell, Trump never even commented about the ad situation.
 Zuckerberg is worried that free speech will only ever ratchet down, and that we’ll regret it someday. “Over time, in general we tend to add more policies to restrict things more and more,” he said. “If every time there’s something that’s controversial your instinct is, okay let’s restrict a lot, then you do end up restricting a lot of things that I think will be eventually good for everyone.”
 Employees I spoke with did not seem particularly moved by these answers. “Everyone’s grateful we have a chance to address these things directly with him,” one told me. “At the same time, no one thinks he gave a single real answer.” Another said Zuckerberg appeared “really scared” on the call. “I think he fears his employees turning on him,” the employee said. “At least that’s what I got from facial expressions and tone.”
 At the same time, another employee told me that Zuckerberg’s decision was supported by the majority of the company, but that people who agreed with it were afraid to speak out for fear of appearing insensitive. (An employee who spoke on the call echoed this point.)
 How you view the controversy over Trump posts ultimately depends, I think, on how you think the next year is going to play out. Some are predicting a return to equilibrium; others are expecting an ongoing escalation of violent rhetoric. Another way of saying this is that there are optimists and pessimists.
 The optimists argue that  social media is neither good nor bad, but simply a powerful new tool for society to use. Its misuses get more attention than its positive uses, but those positive uses — such as drawing attention to the murder of an innocent man by police — ultimately outweigh the negative ones. This year might be a particularly fraught moment, the optimists say, but eventually (and maybe even soon!), things will start getting back to normal and we’ll be glad we preserved our free speech traditions so that movements like Black Lives Matter can continue to use these tools for positive ends. Zuckerberg, in this framing, is an optimist.
 The pessimists argue that social networks are  getting played by bad actors who have gamed it to re-enforce and worsen existing power imbalances. The president, for example, has used these platforms to call on police and the military to “dominate” peaceful protesters and crack down on constitutionally protected gatherings — using his own free speech, coupled with the algorithmic promotion that social platforms to provide, to suppress the free speech of others. The pessimist knows that you can never truly de-platform the president of the United States, but she would rather not help a wealthy and powerful corporation build the megaphone. Especially given that  she knows worse posts are to come, and that the collective damage is likely only to increase.
 The optimist believes that the long arc of history bends toward justice. The pessimist turns on the TV and sees fascist boots on the ground already, tear-gassing their fellowAmericans. The optimist may have the time and privilege to wait out any civil unrest. The pessimist may not.
   ⬆️ Trending up:   Grindr is removing ethnicity filters from its app. The decision is meant to show solidarity with protests in the US over police brutality against black women and men. This is a great step — the ethnicity filters have been the source of countless racist interactions on the platform. (Ashley Carman /  The Verge)
 ⬇️ Trending down:   Google is placing ads on websites that publish coronavirus conspiracy theories, helping their owners generate revenue and continue their operations. In at least one instance, Google has run ads featuring a conspiracist it has already banned. (Ryan Gallagher and Mark Bergen / Bloomberg)
              ⭐ The nonprofit Center for Democracy and Technology filed a lawsuit against  Donald Trump . The group says Trump’s executive order against social media companies violated the companies’ right to free speech. But I thought the president loved free speech! Here’s Kate Conger at the  The New York Times:
 Mr. Trump’s order is “plainly retaliatory,” the Center for Democracy and Technology said in a legal filing. “It attacks a private company, Twitter, for exercising its First Amendment right to comment on the president’s statements.”
 The order could also prevent other companies from speaking freely, the organization argued. “President Trump — by publicly attacking Twitter and issuing the order — sought to chill future online speech by other speakers,” its filing said.
 Twitter has restricted a tweet from Rep. Matt Gaetz (R-FL) for violating its policies against glorifying violence. The news follows a similar action taken against  President  Donald  Trump last week. This tweet is truly disturbing, and I’m glad Twitter acted. (Makena Kelly /  The Verge)
 A narrow majority of Americans believe  Twitter was right to flag one of  President  Trump’s tweets as violating its rules on violence. Unsurprisingly, according to a National Research Group survey, liberals were far more likely to support Twitter, with conservatives more likely to oppose the decision. (Ina Fried /  Axios)
 Donald Trump’s anger at  Twitter is drawing more attention to the platform. That’s good for Twitter’s bottom line. (Sarah Frier / Bloomberg)
 Misinformation about the  George Floyd protests is continuing to surge on  Twitter. Conspiracy theorists are spreading rumors that Floyd is still alive, and that George Soros is funding the protests. (Davey Alba /  The New York Times)
 In the past week,  Donald  Trump’s violent rhetoric has been realized in actual violence across the streets of the country. With he and his allies now threatening a military crackdown, it’s time to ban them from  Facebook and  Twitter, my colleague TC Sottek argues. ( The Verge)
 Misinformation about MSNBC running footage of World War Z instead of real protest images went viral on  Twitter last weekend. One person tweeted it as a joke, then tried to shut it down as the post went viral, but it was already too late. (Adi Robertson /  The Verge)
 A white nationalist organization posing as antifa called for violence on  Twitter . “Tonight we say ‘F--- The City’ and we move into the residential areas... the white hoods.... and we take what’s ours …” they tweeted, in an effort in incite violence at the protests. (Ben Collins, Brandy Zadrozny and Emmanuelle Saliba / NBC)
 A largely peaceful protest in Oakland turned violent at the very end when the police accused protestors of violating curfew. Online, images of the clash dominated the coverage. (Zoe Schiffer /  The Verge)
 The scale of the protests is all-encompassing, writes Charlie Warzel. It’s a singular, collective trauma dominating the national consciousness, with no other channels to watch, and no distractions. (Charlie Warzel /  The New York Times)
   Media outlets that post their own articles on  Facebook are liable for other Facebook users’ defamatory comments on those posts, an Australian court ruled. The media companies that were defendants in the original lawsuit, which include News Corp and the Sydney Morning Herald, said they are considering a further appeal to the country’s highest court. (Mike Cherney /  The Wall Street Journal)
 The  Trump administration said it would open an investigation into taxes on digital commerce that have been adopted in the European Union. The move escalates a global battle that will affect where tech giants like  Facebook and  Amazon pay taxes. (Jim Tankersley and Ana Swanson /  The New York Times)
  ⭐  TikTok is becoming a political platform as users speak out in support of the Black Lives Matter movement. The news comes a year after TikTok was  accused of censoring videos related to the protests in Hong Kong. The company said then that the videos weren’t getting views because they weren’t what users wanted to see. Arriana McLymore and Echo Wang at Reuters have the story:
 The hashtag #blacklivesmatter has shot up TikTok’s trending list with 3 billion views as of Tuesday morning. TikTok superstars like Charli D’Amelio, whose 60 million followers is nearly twice the number of HBO’s U.S. subscribers, hit pause on showing off dance moves to discuss George Floyd, a black man in Minnesota whose death as a white police officer knelt on his neck has sparked a national debate on race and power.
 “I will continue to spread these messages and be an ally,” said D’Amelio, who is white, in a post which garnered more than 47.7 million views and 12 million likes over the weekend.
 “Out of context”  Twitter accounts are turning into protest accounts to support the ongoing movement fighting against police brutality. Creators are listing bail funds for people to donate to and posting advice on safely protesting. (Julia Alexander /  The Verge)
 Spotify,  Apple Music,  Amazon Music, and  YouTube Music have pledged their support for Blackout Tuesday with special playlists, moments of silence, and social media blackouts. The campaign is aimed at protesting police violence and racism as well as honoring  George Floyd. (Nick Statt and Jon Porter /  The Verge)
 Blackout Tuesday posts inadvertently drowned out protest movements by using the #BlackLivesMatter hashtag. It’s a high-stakes example of the limitations of using hashtags to organize. (James Vincent /  The Verge)
 Former  Facebook executive Barry Schnitt wrote an open letter to employees on Medium. “Facebook says, and may even believe, that it is on the side of free speech. In fact, it has put itself on the side of profit and cowardice,” he said. (Barry Schnitt / Medium)
 Google  Translate supports only about 100 languages. During a pandemic, when COVID PSAs need to be translated into as many languages as possible, in ways that are accurate and culturally appropriate, that’s a problem. (Gretchen McCulloch /  Wired)
 Zoom revenue grew 169 percent this quarter. The company also doubled its revenue guidance for the year. (Jordan Novet / CNBC)
   Donate to support the fight for justice. I promise you’ll feel better after you do; this link has lots of ideas.
 Read this thread about anti-racism. It’s geared toward educators, but I got a lot out of it.
  the CEO of Zoom, Eric Yuan, just started talking on his company's blowout earnings call on Zoom and he was on mute  pic.twitter.com/NV0VQ14SEQ
— Jordan Novet (@jordannovet)  June 2, 2020
 me (posting the black square): solidarity  *the black square gets owned by every single black person i know online*  me (deleting the black square): solidarity
— Rajat Suresh (@rajat_suresh)  June 2, 2020
 This is NOT what America is about. America is about *opens history book*  uh oh  *Frantically starts flipping though pages*  uh oh. oh no. no no no. uh oh
— Grosdoriane (@Grosdoriane)  May 24, 2019
title: 我们从泄露的马克·扎克伯格面对员工的音频中学到了九件事
title_en: Nine things we learned from leaked audio of Mark Zuckerberg facing his employees

note:
我相信，每个人都有很好的理由亲自投入，在自己的工作场所实现更大的多样性和包容性。
互联网上已经有大量精彩的评论，回顾了科技行业存在重大差距的证据。与其重复那些相同的发现，我想要支持将同理心作为多样性和包容性的推动力。用梅里亚姆-韦伯斯特的话来说，移情是理解一个人的感觉、思想和经历的行为，而不依赖于明确的交流或自己的经历。如果我们在我们的行业中促进同理心作为核心价值，我们将激发成功和创新，同时自然地激励人们参与成功的多样性和包容性倡议。
我做了不到5年的软件开发。在我短暂的职业生涯中，我曾在两家公司工作过，包括我目前在Indigo Ag的职位。在此之前，我在一家中型科技公司工作，既做前端开发，也做后端开发。我在大学三年级和四年级之间从实习生开始，毕业后加入了全职工作。我非常符合软件开发人员的刻板印象：我是一个30多岁的白人，来自一个舒适的中产阶级郊区，受过文科大学教育，在调查和民意调查中几乎勾选了每一个“多数”人口统计数字框。希望这个背景能让我对我即将分享的观点、经验和想法的局限性有一些洞察力。
在最初的几年里，我在一旁看着多样性和包容性倡议来来去去。我完成了必修的培训模块。我偶尔会阅读办公室里流传的专栏或博客文章。我收到了人力资源部发来的关于新员工组寻找成员的电子邮件。表面上，我同意公平和机会平等的基本概念，但在内心深处，我并不是真的相信为什么我们需要促进多样性。相当多的女性在开发团队工作，还有一些有色人种，她们似乎没有一个比我处于劣势。我认为多元化规划是公司自上而下规定的一种形式，与通常的合规培训和参与度调查并驾齐驱。我点头同意，没有在这些倡议上投入任何精力，因为我个人对它们的结果没有任何利害关系。至少我以为我没有。
我在公司工作的第三年，我对现状的接受突然被打破了。我们的高管团队正在就潜在的收购-合并进行深入谈判，当时一家媒体发表了一篇关于一项长达十年的法律和解的报道，其中一名创始人目前担任首席执行官。此案的核心是针对其当时配偶的家庭暴力事件，这一事件已得到双方的确认。研发部门的每个人，以及公司的其他部门，都对此事发表了意见，有些人选择公开表达这些意见。一些人要求高管辞职，而另一些人则反对因相关各方已经解决的过去私事而惩罚某人。
在这场辩论中，一些员工对公司领导层和工程领导层处理这种情况的方式提出了警告。据我所知，这些员工大多是女性。他们声称，领导层优先考虑高管和公司的形象和声誉，而不是受事件造成的环境伤害的员工的需求。这个立场一开始让我迷惑不解。当然，经理们会小心翼翼地绕过这场争议。他们当然不会要求解雇创始人。我们还能对他们有什么更高的期望呢？那么这种情况对这些员工如此不利又是怎么回事呢？在我看来，他们总是可以选择退出办公室政治，像我一样专注于自己的工作。
随着戏剧性的拖延，一些与我密切合作的开发人员变得越来越与他们的工作和团队脱节。他们在全体会议上明显感到沮丧，午餐时互相发泄。同时，创始人表示道歉，并对自己的行为表示遗憾。他继续在公司会议上发言。他脸上的松弛表情符号，在消息传出之前很受欢迎，现在仍然经常使用。虽然一些员工开始引用在会议和会议上对女性的不当行为事件，但其他与他密切合作多年的人却谈到并写道，他是一个多么模范的领导者。到他辞职时-公司大张旗鼓-办公室的紧张气氛显而易见。
对于许多曾对领导的反应持批评态度的员工来说，这次离职太少、太晚了。一些人离开了，而另一些人仍然空闲着。突然，我意识到这种情况对我喜欢与之共事的人有多大的影响，这些人新发现的愤世嫉俗和愤怒也影响着我今天的生活。我变得更加投入到理解中去。
当我这样表达这个问题时，突然间它变得显而易见：如果我过去经历过家庭暴力或虐待，就会完全改变我处理这种情况的方式。我以前也有朋友跟我分享过被虐待的经历，但我从来没有在知情的情况下目睹过心爱的人遭受虐待，也没有被亲近的人虐待过。我尽我所能想象，亲身或间接体验虐待可能造成的伤害和痛苦，然后了解到一位值得信赖和尊敬的领导人给配偶造成了那种创伤，会是什么感觉？当我每天都要看到这个人的脸，听到他们谈论团队合作以及我们是如何团结在一起的时候，我怎么可能专注于工作，听别人赞扬他们的领导能力。我可以像什么都没发生一样每天工作吗？我不能肯定地说我可以。
当我考虑这一观点的含义时，大多数对领导层的反应感到不安的人都是女性，这一事实开始变得很有意义。从统计上看，女性比男性更有可能经历过家庭暴力或虐待。在新闻制造的衰弱环境中，这些员工并不是简单地寻求报应正义。他们从同事和工作场所领导那里寻求急需的支持，但他们没有找到。如果我们看看情况的另一面，大多数因缺乏支持而受到批评的决策者都是男性。从统计数据上看，他们不太可能成为家庭暴力的受害者，这让他们陷入了与我相同的境地。我怀疑他们中的大多数人都没有意识到与受影响最严重的人团结一致的紧迫性和重要性。
在接下来的几个月里，我强调了更多地关注与多样性和包容性相关的讨论、文章和活动。当我在办公室遇到更具包容性的新方法时，我决定自己尝试一下。我遇到的一件事是研究表明，男性比其他男性更经常打断女性，所以我开始密切关注会议。我试图注意到什么时候我要切断某人的电话，这样我就可以让他们完成他们的想法，或者至少承认我的错误。我还寻找某人被另一位参与者打断的例子，在这一点上，我会试图将对话引回到他们身上，这样他们就可以继续他们的观点。我没有对此进行量化，所以我不能肯定地告诉你，我是否观察到女性比男性更频繁地被切断联系。我确实观察到的是，这种行为减少了见面时的敌意，使达成共识变得更容易。这是我关于促进基于同理心的包容行为的价值的第一课。通过这些行为，我们学会了识别和补救我们的队友被剥夺参与和贡献机会的情况。
这样的行为还有很多其他的例子。许多科技行业的女性报告说，她们发现自己承担着做笔记等行政任务的责任。当我注意到当时我的团队中唯一的女性开发人员也是唯一做笔记的人时，我私下问她是否感到有责任。她说，不，她并不觉得被迫担任这个角色，但如果我想在会议上分担做笔记的责任，她肯定不会反对。当我们这样做的时候，她不仅得到了休息，而且我发现自己的注意力更集中了。
中性代词可能是一个特别热门的话题，特别是当涉及到成语“男人”时。如果你问不同性别的人对“Guys”作为中性代词的用法有何看法，你会得到从漠不关心、恼火到冒犯的各种答案。上下文在这里很重要，因为“嘿，伙计们”这句话通常被认为没有“IT家伙”那么富有。我承认我仍然在使用前者，但我已经尽了最大努力在工作中不再使用“插入团队”这样的短语，即使是在提到一个全是男性的团队时也是如此。因为开发团队成员经常来来去去，习惯使用包容性语言是向新成员发出从第一天起就欢迎他们的信号的一种很好的方式。
同理心鼓励人们采取我刚才描述的行为。感同身受的心态鼓励我们质疑为什么人们对某些言语、行为或情况的反应与我们不同。这个人是不是经常说不完他们最后一个团队的一句话？他们是否已经筋疲力尽，不得不在每次会议上都做笔记，但又不愿停下来，因为从来没有人收拾残局？他们有没有因为不是“男人中的一员”而被以前的公司排斥过？
尽管这些个人行为改变可能是有益的，但多样性和包容性倡议不能止步于此。在技术行业内部，在招聘、工作满意度、薪酬和晋升方面存在着明显的差距，这是不可能的。
为了避免这种情况，我们需要对招聘和晋升等长期流程进行系统性改革。这不是一件容易的事。它需要公司各级员工愿意与同事打交道，对自己诚实，敢于冒险，敢于犯错。必须克服的一个共同反对意见是坚持(尤其是领导层)将政治排除在工作场所之外。这一政策背后肯定有一定的道理。政治方面，比如对病毒式社交媒体或选举候选人的讨论，在一定程度上脱离了对工作场所重要价值观的讨论。多样性和包容性更具相关性，不应被列入禁区话题类别。
这就是同理心弥合分歧的地方。同理心帮助人们理解，他们的女同性恋同事并不是在推动办公室骄傲事件引起关注，而是因为她想在午餐时毫无畏惧地兴奋地谈论她的婚礼，就像她的异性恋同事一样。或者，当他们要求你使用某些代词时，他们的跨性别团队成员并不难，因为这样做可以解放他们的思维，让他们专注于团队紧迫的最后期限。或者，在评判应聘者的着装和发型时，选择考虑文化规范的差异，并不是试图让白人申请者处于不利地位，而是试图招募其他公司拒绝的优秀新同事。对于受影响的人来说，这些不是政治问题，而是每天跟随他们工作的真正问题，影响他们的表现和健康。这些问题应该在各个层面得到解决，从个人到团队，再到首席执行官。
我们很多人习惯于相信，如果我们想在软件开发这样竞争激烈的行业取得成功，我们必须忍受任何逆境，不要再将自己的情况与同事的情况进行比较。换句话说，生活是不公平的--面对它。
这是个错误。诚然，科技行业有很多方面是我们无法控制的：抢先进入市场的压力，我们用户不断变化的需求，安全性和易用性之间的微妙平衡。但是，我们选择的说话、工作、招聘、评估和提升彼此的方式并不是这些外部性之一。这完全在我们的控制之下。此外，多样性有多种形式，从我们因外表而被贴上标签的方式，到对我们与他人的关系的判断，再到从我们的大脑和身体的运作方式得出的结论。有些人淡化了保护他人免受偏见和歧视的必要性，他们自己可能就是偏见的无知受害者。
为了真正致力于推动技术向前发展，我们应该愿意改变我们的行为和我们的系统，使每个人，无论背景或身份，都能向我们的团队展示他们的价值，为我们的使命贡献最大的力量，并分享我们团队的成功。就像我们的编程语言、我们的部署管道和我们的设计范例一样，我们应该不断地评估和迭代我们彼此之间的思维、语言和行动模式。我们从来不认为我们的软件是完美的，那么为什么要以不同的方式对待我们的文化呢？
note_en:
I believe everyone has good reasons to be personally invested in achieving greater diversity and inclusion in their workplace.
 There are already plenty of great write-ups on the internet reviewing the evidence that major disparities exist across the tech industry. Instead of repeating those same findings, I want to argue in favor of empathy as a driving force for diversity and inclusion. To paraphrase Merriam-Webster, empathy is the action of understanding a person’s feelings, thoughts, and experiences without relying on explicit communication or one’s own experiences. If we promote empathy as a core value within our industry, we will spark success and innovation while naturally motivating people to engage in successful diversity and inclusion initiatives.
 I’ve worked as a software developer for just under 5 years. I’ve worked at two companies in my short career, including my current role at Indigo Ag. Before then, I worked at a mid-sized tech company doing both front- and back-end development. I started as an intern between my junior and senior years of college and joined full-time after graduation. I very much fit the software developer stereotype: I’m a white guy in his late twenties from a comfortable middle-class suburb with a liberal arts college education who ticks pretty much every “majority” demographics box on surveys and polls. Hopefully, this background offers some insight into the limits of the opinions, experiences, and ideas I’m about to share.
 For the first couple of years, I watched from the sidelines as diversity and inclusion initiatives came and went. I completed the mandatory training modules. I read the occasional op-ed or blog post circulating around the office. I received the emails from HR about new employee groups looking for members. Outwardly, I agreed with the basic concepts of fairness and equal opportunity, but deep down I wasn’t really sold on why we needed to promote diversity. Quite a few women worked on the development team, as well as a few people of color, and none of them appeared to be at a disadvantage relative to me. I viewed diversity programming as a formality dictated from above by corporate alongside the usual compliance training and engagement surveys. I nodded along without investing any energy in these initiatives because I had no personal stake in their outcomes. At least, I thought I didn’t.
 My acceptance of the status quo was abruptly shattered somewhere into my third year at the company. Our executive teams were deep into negotiating a potential acquisition-merger when a media outlet published a report on a decade-old legal settlement involving one of the founders, who was currently a chief executive. The case centered on incidents of domestic abuse against his then-spouse which had been confirmed by both parties. Everyone in R&D, as well as the rest of the company, had opinions on the matter, and some chose to express those opinions publicly. Some called for the executive to resign, while others objected to punishing someone for a past private affair that the involved parties had resolved.
 Amidst this debate, a number of employees admonished both corporate and engineering leadership for the way they were handling the situation. From what I could tell, most of these employees were women. They claimed that leadership was prioritizing the executive’s and the company’s image and reputation over the needs of employees harmed by the environment that the incident had created. This stance confused me at first. Naturally, managers were going to tiptoe around the controversy. They certainly weren’t going to call for a founder to be fired. What more could we expect from them? And what about this situation was so detrimental to these employees? In my mind, they could always opt-out of the office politics and focus on their work like I was doing.
 As the drama dragged on, some of the developers I worked closely with became more and more disengaged with their work and their teams. They became visibly frustrated at all-hands meetings and vented to each other at lunch. Meanwhile, the founder apologized and expressed regret for his actions. He continued to speak at company meetings. Slack emojis of his face, which were popular prior to the news breaking, remained in regular use. While some employees began citing incidents of inappropriate behavior towards women at meetings and conferences, others who had worked closely with him for many years spoke and wrote about what an exemplary leader he was. By the time he resigned — too much corporate fanfare — the tension in the office was palpable.
 For many of the employees who had been critical of how their leaders had responded, this departure was too little, too late. Some left, while others remained unengaged. Suddenly I realized how much the situation had affected people who I enjoyed working with, people whose newfound cynicism and anger were also impacting on my own day today. I became far more invested in understanding why they were burdened so heavily by a situation that hadn’t affected me substantially. I asked myself: What could have led me to react so strongly to the news that a leader at my workplace had committed an act of domestic violence?
 When I phrased the question that way, suddenly it was obvious: an experience with domestic violence or abuse in my past would have completely changed how I dealt with this situation. I have had friends share past experiences of abuse to me before, but I’ve never knowingly witnessed a loved one suffer through an abusive relationship, nor have I been abused by someone close to me. I imagined as best I could, what it would be like to have first or second-hand experience of the damage and pain abuse can cause, and then learn that a trusted and respected leader inflicted that kind of trauma on a spouse? How could I possibly focus on work when I have to see this person’s face every day, hear them talking about teamwork and how we are all in this together, and listen to others praise their leadership. Could I go about my work each day as though nothing had happened? I can’t say with confidence that I could.
 As I considered the implications of this perspective, the fact that most of those upset about leadership’s response were women started to make a lot of sense. Women are statistically more likely than men to have experienced domestic violence or abuse. Amidst the debilitating environment created by the news, these employees were not simply seeking retributive justice. They were looking for much-needed support from their coworkers and workplace leaders, and they did not find it. If we look at the other side of the situation, most of the decision-makers being criticized for their lack of support were men. Statistically, they were less likely to have been victim to domestic violence, landing them in the same situation as me. I suspect most of them were unaware of the urgency and importance of demonstrating solidarity with the people most affected.
 In the following months, I made a point to pay more attention to discussions, articles, and events related to diversity and inclusion. When I came across new ways to be more inclusive in the office, I decided to try them out for myself. One thing I encountered were studies suggesting men interrupt women more often than they did other men, so I started paying close attention at meetings. I tried to notice when I was about to cut someone off so I could let them finish their thought or at least acknowledge my mistake. I also looked for instances where someone was interrupted by another participant, at which point I would try to guide the conversation back to them so they could resume their point. I did not approach this quantitatively, so I can’t tell you for certain whether I observed women being cut off more frequently than men. What I did observe was that this behavior reduced meeting hostility and made it easier to come to a consensus. This was my first lesson about the value of promoting empathy-based inclusive behavior. Through these behaviors, we learn to identify and remedy situations where our teammates are being denied opportunities to participate and contribute.
 There are plenty of other examples of such behaviors. Many women in tech report that they find themselves assuming responsibility for administrative tasks such as note-taking. When I observed that the sole woman developer on my team at the time was also the only one taking notes, I asked her privately about whether she felt obligated. She said that no, she hadn’t felt forced into the role, but that if I wanted to share the responsibility of note-taking at meetings she certainly wouldn’t object. When we did, not only did she get a break, but I found myself paying better attention.
 Gender-neutral pronouns can be a particularly hot topic, especially when it comes to the idiom “guys.” If you ask people of different genders their opinions on the use of “guys” as a neutral pronoun, you’ll get answers ranging from indifference to annoyance to offense. Context matters here, as the phrase “hey guys,” is often seen as less loaded than “the IT guys.” I’ll admit to still using the former, but I’ve tried my hardest to stop using phrases like “the [insert team] guys” at work, even in reference to a team that is all men. Because development team members come and go regularly, habitual use of inclusive language is a great way to signal to new members that they are welcome from day one.
 Empathy encourages the adoption of behaviors like the ones I just described. An empathetic mindset encourages us to question the reasons people respond to certain words, actions, or situations differently than we do. Was this person constantly unable to finish a sentence on their last team? Are they exhausted having to take notes every meeting, but reluctant to stop because no one ever picks up the slack? Have they ever been ostracized at a prior company for not being “one of the guys?”
 As beneficial as these individual changes in behavior can be, diversity and inclusion initiatives cannot stop there. There are clear disparities present in hiring, job satisfaction, compensation, and promotion within the tech industry that cannot be explained by innate differences between people of different demographics groups. Even in instances where genetics may have some small influence on career interests, it is insufficient to entirely explain the observed gaps. This means we must look to the culture and history of the environments where we work and live to understand where these disparities emerge.
 When we operate on the belief that our workplace is already free of bias and discrimination, we fail to recognize the expressed frustrations of our minority teammates as indicators of a sub-optimal work environment. Instead, we silently suggest that those teammates are not capable enough or resilient enough to work with us. We also signal distrust when we question the motives behind their efforts to change the way our workplace functions. Trust in each other’s intentions is a prerequisite for a workplace conducive to collaboration, and a lack of trust should be a red flag to everyone.
 To avoid this, we need to embrace systemic reforms of longstanding processes like hiring and promotion. This is not an easy task. It requires a willingness from people at all levels of a company to engage with their coworkers, be honest with themselves, take chances, and make mistakes. One common objection that must be overcome is the insistence, especially by leadership, to keep politics out of the workplace. There is definitely some truth behind this policy. Aspects of politics like discussions of viral social media or election candidates are degrees removed from discussions about values that matter in the workplace. Diversity and inclusion are far more relevant and should not be included in the category of off-limits topics.
 This is where empathy bridges the divide. Empathy helps people understand that their lesbian coworker isn’t pushing an office PRIDE event for attention, but because she wants to talk excitedly at lunch about her wedding without fear, the same way her straight coworkers do. Or that their trans team member isn’t being difficult when they ask you to use certain pronouns, because doing so frees their mind to focus on your team’s tight deadlines. Or that choosing to accommodate differences in cultural norms when judging an interviewee’s attire and hairstyle is not an attempt to disadvantage white applicants, but an attempt to recruit awesome new coworkers that other companies passed up. These are not political issues for the people they impact, they are real issues that follow them to work every day, affecting their performance and health. They deserve to be addressed at every level, from individuals to teams to chief executives.
 Many of us are conditioned to believe that if we want to succeed in a highly competitive industry like software development, we have to endure whatever adversity is thrown our way and stop comparing our own situation to our colleagues’. In other words, life’s not fair — deal with it.
 This is a mistake. True, there are many aspects of the tech industry that we cannot control: the pressure to be first to market, the ever-changing demands of our users, the delicate balance between security and ease of use. But the way we choose to speak, work, hire, evaluate, and promote one another is not one of those externalities. It is entirely in our control. Furthermore, diversity takes many forms, from the ways we are labeled due to our appearance, to the judgments cast on our relationships with others, to the conclusions drawn from the way our brains and bodies function. Some of those who downplay the need to protect others from bias and discrimination may be unknowing victims of bias themselves.
 To truly commit to driving technology forward, we should be willing to change our behaviors and our systems in ways that enable everyone, regardless of background or identity, to demonstrate their value to our team, contribute their best to our mission, and share in our team’s success. Just like with our programming languages, our deployment pipelines, and our design paradigms, we should constantly assess and iterate our modes of thought, speech, and action towards one another. We never assume our software is perfect, so why treat our culture any differently?
title: 练习移情可以教会我们科技多元化和包容性的价值。
title_en: Practicing empathy can teach us the value of diversity and inclusion in tech

贝叶斯非参数模型是参数数量随提供的数据量自由增长的模型；因此，不是训练几个复杂度不同的模型并对它们进行比较，而是能够设计一个其复杂度随着观察到的更多数据而增长的模型。实践中贝叶斯非参数的典型例子是Dirichlet过程混合模型(DPMM)。DPMM允许从业者在其数据的几何结构中的不同簇的数量未知的情况下构建混合模型-换句话说，允许随着观察到的数据的增多而增长簇的数量。这一特性使得DPMM对于探索性数据分析非常有用，在探索性数据分析中，我们几乎不知道有问题的数据的各个方面；本演示文稿旨在演示这一事实。
Dirichlet过程是离散概率分布上的一族概率分布族。形式上，Dirichlet过程(DP)由某种基本概率分布\(G_0：\ω\to\mathbb{R}\)和一个通常表示为\(\α)的正的实数标度参数来指定。参数为\(G_0：\omega\to\mathbb{R}\)和\(\α\)的Dirichlet过程的样本\(G\)本身是\(\Ω\)上的分布。对于\(\Omega\)的任何不相交分区\(\Omega_1，.，\Omega_k\)和任何样本\(G\sim DP(G_0，\alpha)\)，我们有：
本质上，这是对样本空间\(\Ω\)进行离散划分，然后使用基础分布\(G_0\)在其上构建离散分布。虽然Dirichlet过程在公式上相当抽象，但在各种图形模型中作为先验是非常有用的。在下面的方案中，这一事实变得更容易看到。
想象一下，一家餐厅有无限张桌子(由正整数索引)，一次只接受一位顾客。第(N)位客户根据以下概率选择座位：
以概率\(\frac{n_t}{\alpha+n-1}\)坐在\(t\)桌，其中\(n_t\)是在桌\(t\)的人数。
如果我们从欧米茄上的基分布(G_0)中关联一个抽签给每个表\(t\)，然后将非正规化概率质量\(n_t\)关联到该抽签上，则得到的欧米茄上的分布等价于狄利克雷过程的抽签(DP(G_0，α)\)。
此外，我们可以很容易地将其扩展到定义非参数混合模型的生成过程：每个表\(T)至少有一个客户席位与一组聚类参数\(\theta_t\)相关联，这些参数本身来自于某个基分布\(G0\)。对于每个新的观测值，首先根据上述概率将该观测值分配到一个表中；然后，从该表的聚类参数参数化的分布中提取该观测值。如果观测被分配到新的表，则从\(G_0\)中绘制一组新的群集参数，然后从由这些群集参数参数化的分布中绘制观测。
虽然Dirichlet过程混合模型的这种表述是直观的，但在概率规划框架中也很难对其进行推理。这激发了DPMM的另一种表述，经验表明，这种表述更有利于推理(例如，Blee和Jordan，2004年)。
对于每个观测值\(n\in\{1，.，N\}\)，绘制\(z_n\sim\pi(\beta_{1：\infty})\)，然后绘制\(x_n\sim f(\theta_{z_n})\)。
这里，可以更容易地看到Dirichlet过程混合模型的无限本质。此外，所有的\(\beta_i\)都是独立的，因此在概率编程框架中执行推理要容易得多。

上面的曲线图显示了缩放超参数\(\α\)的影响。较大的\(\alpha\)会产生更厚尾的权重分布，而较小的\(\alpha\)会在较少的星团上放置更多质量。特别地，中间的群集看起来可以生成单个高斯(尽管实际上它是由两个不同的高斯生成的)，因此\(\α\)的设置允许实践者进一步编码他们关于数据包含多少群集的先验信念。
如前所述，当探索潜在几何结构完全未知的数据集时，Dirichlet过程混合模型确实大放异彩。为了证明这一点，我们根据过去300年的太阳黑子计数数据(由比利时皇家天文台提供)拟合了DPMM：

Bayesian nonparametric models are models where the number of parameters grow freely with the amount of data provided; thus, instead of training several models that vary in complexity and comparing them, one is able to design a model whose complexity grows as more data are observed. The prototypical example of Bayesian nonparametrics in practice is the  Dirichlet Process Mixture Model (DPMM). A DPMM allows for a practitioner to build a mixture model when the number of distinct clusters in thegeometric structure of their data is unknown – in other words, the number of clusters is allowed to grow as more data is observed. This feature makes the DPMM highly useful towards exploratory data analysis, where few facets of the data in question are known; this presentation aims to demonstrate this fact.
 Dirichlet processes are a family of probability distributions over discrete probability distributions. Formally, the Dirichlet process (DP) is specified by some base probability distribution  \(G_0: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}\) and a positive, real, scaling parameter commonly denoted as  \(\alpha\). A sample  \(G\) from a Dirichlet process with parameters  \(G_0: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}\) and  \(\alpha\) is itself a distribution over  \(\Omega\). For any disjoint partition \(\Omega_1, ..., \Omega_k\) of  \(\Omega\), and any sample  \(G \sim DP(G_0, \alpha)\), we have:
  Essentially, this is taking a discrete partition of our sample space  \(\Omega\) and subsequently constructing a discrete distribution over it using the base distribution  \(G_0\). While quite abstract in formulation, the Dirichlet process is very useful as a prior in various graphical models. This fact becomes easier to see in the following scheme.
 Imagine a restaurant with infinite tables (indexed by the positive integers) that accepts customers one at a time. The  \(n\)th customer chooses their seat according to the following probabilities:
 With probability  \(\frac{n_t}{\alpha + n - 1}\), sit at table  \(t\), where  \(n_t\) is the number of people at table  \(t\)
 If we associate to each table  \(t\) a draw from a base distribution  \(G_0\) over  \(\Omega\), and then associate unnormalized probability mass  \(n_t\) to that draw, the resulting distribution over  \(\Omega\) is equivalent to a draw from a Dirichlet process  \(DP(G_0, \alpha)\).
 Furthermore, we can easily extend this to define the generative process of a nonparametric mixture model: every table  \(t\) that has at least one customer seated is associated with a set of cluster parameters  \(\theta_t\), which were themselves drawn from some base distribution  \(G_0\). For each new observation, first assign that observation to a table according to the above probabilities; then, that observation is drawn from the distribution parameterized by the cluster parametersfor that table. If the observation was assigned to a new table, draw a new set of cluster parameters from  \(G_0\), and then draw the observation from the distribution parameterized by those cluster parameters.
 While this formulation of a Dirichlet process mixture model is intuitive, it is also very difficult to perform inference on in a probabilistic programming framework. This motivates an alternative formulation of DPMMs, which has empirically been shown to be more conducive to inference (e.g. Blei and Jordan, 2004).
 For each observation  \(n \in \{1, ..., N\}\), draw  \(z_n \sim \pi(\beta_{1:\infty})\), and then draw  \(x_n \sim f(\theta_{z_n})\)
 Here, the infinite nature of the Dirichlet process mixture model can more easily be seen. Furthermore, all  \(\beta_i\) are independent, so it is far easier to perform inference in a probabilistic programming framework.
  import torchfrom torch.distributions import constraintsimport torch.nn.functional as Fimport pandas as pdimport numpy as npimport matplotlib.pyplot as pltimport matplotlib.cm as cmfrom tqdm import tqdmfrom pyro.distributions import *import pyrofrom pyro.optim import Adamfrom pyro.infer import SVI, Trace_ELBO, Predictiveassert pyro.__version__.startswith('1')pyro.enable_validation(True) # can help with debuggingpyro.set_rng_seed(0)

 We begin by demonstrating the capabilities of Dirichlet process mixture models on a synthetic dataset generated by a mixture of four 2D Gaussians:
 data = torch.cat((MultivariateNormal(-8 * torch.ones(2), torch.eye(2)).sample([50]), MultivariateNormal(8 * torch.ones(2), torch.eye(2)).sample([50]), MultivariateNormal(torch.tensor([1.5, 2]), torch.eye(2)).sample([50]), MultivariateNormal(torch.tensor([-0.5, 1]), torch.eye(2)).sample([50])))plt.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1])plt.title("Data Samples from Mixture of 4 Gaussians")plt.show()N = data.shape[0]
  In this example, the cluster parameters  \(\theta_i\) are two dimensional vectors describing the means of a multivariate Gaussian with identity covariance. Therefore, the Dirichlet process base distribution  \(G_0\) is also a multivariate Gaussian (i.e. the conjugate prior), although this choice is not as computationally useful, since we are not performing coordinate-ascent variational inference but rather black-box variational inference using Pyro.
 First, let’s define the “stick-breaking” function that generates our weights, given our samples of  \(\beta\):
 def mix_weights(beta): beta1m_cumprod = (1 - beta).cumprod(-1) return F.pad(beta, (0, 1), value=1) * F.pad(beta1m_cumprod, (1, 0), value=1)
 Next, let’s define our model. It may be helpful to refer the definition of the stick-breaking model presented in the first part of this tutorial.
 Note that all  \(\beta_i\) samples are conditionally independent, so we model them using a   pyro.plate of size   T-1; we do the same for all samples of our cluster parameters  \(\mu_i\). We then construct a Categorical distribution whose parameters are the mixture weights using our sampled  \(\beta\) values (line 9) below, and sample the cluster assignment  \(z_n\) for each data point from that Categorical. Finally, we sample our observations from a multivariate Gaussiandistribution whose mean is exactly the cluster parameter corresponding to the assignment  \(z_n\) we drew for the point  \(x_n\). This can be seen in the Pyro code below:
 def model(data): with pyro.plate("beta_plate", T-1): beta = pyro.sample("beta", Beta(1, alpha)) with pyro.plate("mu_plate", T): mu = pyro.sample("mu", MultivariateNormal(torch.zeros(2), 5 * torch.eye(2))) with pyro.plate("data", N): z = pyro.sample("z", Categorical(mix_weights(beta))) pyro.sample("obs", MultivariateNormal(mu[z], torch.eye(2)), obs=data)
  The variational family  \(q(\beta, \theta, z)\) that we are optimizing over during variational inference is given by:
  Note that since we are unable to computationally model the infinite clusters posited by the model, we truncate our variational family at  \(T\) clusters. This does not affect our model; rather, it is a simplification made in the  inference stage to allow tractability.
 The guide is constructed exactly according to the definition of our variational family  \(q(\beta, \theta, z)\) above. We have  \(T-1\) conditionally independent Beta distributions for each  \(\beta\) sampled in our model,  \(T\) conditionally independent multivariate Gaussians for each cluster parameter  \(\mu_i\), and  \(N\) conditionally independent Categorical distributions for each cluster assignment  \(z_n\).
 Our variational parameters (  pyro.param) are therefore the  \(T-1\) many positive scalars that parameterize the second parameter of our variational Beta distributions (the first shape parameter is fixed at  \(1\), as in the model definition), the  \(T\) many two-dimensional vectors that parameterize our variational multivariate Gaussian distributions (we do not parameterize the covariance matrices of the Gaussians, though this should be done when analyzing a real-world dataset formore flexibility), and the  \(N\) many  \(T\)-dimensional vectors that parameterize our variational Categorical distributions:
 def guide(data): kappa = pyro.param('kappa', lambda: Uniform(0, 2).sample([T-1]), constraint=constraints.positive) tau = pyro.param('tau', lambda: MultivariateNormal(torch.zeros(2), 3 * torch.eye(2)).sample([T])) phi = pyro.param('phi', lambda: Dirichlet(1/T * torch.ones(T)).sample([N]), constraint=constraints.simplex) with pyro.plate("beta_plate", T-1): q_beta = pyro.sample("beta", Beta(torch.ones(T-1), kappa)) with pyro.plate("mu_plate", T): q_mu = pyro.sample("mu", MultivariateNormal(tau, torch.eye(2))) with pyro.plate("data", N): z = pyro.sample("z", Categorical(phi))
 When performing inference, we set our ‘guess’ for the maximum number of clusters in the dataset to  \(T = 6\). We define the optimization algorithm (  pyro.optim.Adam) along with the Pyro SVI object and train the model for 1000 iterations.
 After performing inference, we construct the Bayes estimators of the means (the expected values of each factor in our variational approximation) and plot them in red on top of the original dataset. Note that we also have we removed any clusters that have less than a certain weight assigned to them according to our learned variational distributions, and then re-normalize the weights so that they sum to one:
 T = 6optim = Adam({"lr": 0.05})svi = SVI(model, guide, optim, loss=Trace_ELBO())losses = []def train(num_iterations): pyro.clear_param_store() for j in tqdm(range(num_iterations)): loss = svi.step(data) losses.append(loss)def truncate(alpha, centers, weights): threshold = alpha**-1 / 100. true_centers = centers[weights > threshold] true_weights = weights[weights > threshold] / torch.sum(weights[weights > threshold]) return true_centers, true_weightsalpha = 0.1train(1000)# We make a point-estimate of our model parameters using the posterior means of tau and phi for the centers and weightsBayes_Centers_01, Bayes_Weights_01 = truncate(alpha, pyro.param("tau").detach(), torch.mean(pyro.param("phi").detach(), dim=0))alpha = 1.5train(1000)# We make a point-estimate of our model parameters using the posterior means of tau and phi for the centers and weightsBayes_Centers_15, Bayes_Weights_15 = truncate(alpha, pyro.param("tau").detach(), torch.mean(pyro.param("phi").detach(), dim=0))plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)plt.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], color="blue")plt.scatter(Bayes_Centers_01[:, 0], Bayes_Centers_01[:, 1], color="red")plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)plt.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], color="blue")plt.scatter(Bayes_Centers_15[:, 0], Bayes_Centers_15[:, 1], color="red")plt.tight_layout()plt.show()
 100%|██████████| 1000/1000 [00:06<00:00, 153.93it/s]100%|██████████| 1000/1000 [00:06<00:00, 152.39it/s]
   The plots above demonstrate the effects of the scaling hyperparameter  \(\alpha\). A greater  \(\alpha\) yields a more heavy-tailed distribution of the weights, whereas smaller  \(\alpha\) will place more mass on fewer clusters. In particular, the middle cluster looks like it could be generated a single Gaussian (although in fact it was generated by two distinct Gaussians), and thus the setting of  \(\alpha\) allows the practitioner to further encode their prior beliefs about howmany clusters the data contains.
 As mentioned earlier, the Dirichlet process mixture model truly shines when exploring a dataset whose latent geometric structure is completely unknown. To demonstrate this, we fit a DPMM on sunspot count data taken over the past 300 years (provided by the Royal Observatory of Belgium):
 df = pd.read_csv('http://www.sidc.be/silso/DATA/SN_y_tot_V2.0.csv', sep=';', names=['time', 'sunspot.year'], usecols=[0, 1])data = torch.tensor(df['sunspot.year'].values, dtype=torch.float32)N = data.shape[0]plt.hist(df['sunspot.year'].values, bins=40)plt.title("Number of Years vs. Sunspot Counts")plt.xlabel("Sunspot Count")plt.ylabel("Number of Years")plt.show()
  For this example, the cluster parameters  \(\theta_i\) are rate parameters since we are constructing a scale-mixture of Poisson distributions. Again,  \(G_0\) is chosen to be the conjugate prior, which in this case is a Gamma distribution, though this still does not strictly matter for doing inference through Pyro. Below is the implementation of the model:
 def model(data): with pyro.plate("beta_plate", T-1): beta = pyro.sample("beta", Beta(1, alpha)) with pyro.plate("lambda_plate", T): lmbda = pyro.sample("lambda", Gamma(3, 0.05)) with pyro.plate("data", N): z = pyro.sample("z", Categorical(mix_weights(beta))) pyro.sample("obs", Poisson(lmbda[z]), obs=data)def guide(data): kappa = pyro.param('kappa', lambda: Uniform(0, 2).sample([T-1]), constraint=constraints.positive) tau_0 = pyro.param('tau_0', lambda: Uniform(0, 5).sample([T]), constraint=constraints.positive) tau_1 = pyro.param('tau_1', lambda: LogNormal(-1, 1).sample([T]), constraint=constraints.positive) phi = pyro.param('phi', lambda: Dirichlet(1/T * torch.ones(T)).sample([N]), constraint=constraints.simplex) with pyro.plate("beta_plate", T-1): q_beta = pyro.sample("beta", Beta(torch.ones(T-1), kappa)) with pyro.plate("lambda_plate", T): q_lambda = pyro.sample("lambda", Gamma(tau_0, tau_1)) with pyro.plate("data", N): z = pyro.sample("z", Categorical(phi))T = 20alpha = 1.1n_iter = 1500optim = Adam({"lr": 0.05})svi = SVI(model, guide, optim, loss=Trace_ELBO())losses = []train(n_iter)samples = torch.arange(0, 300).type(torch.float)tau0_optimal = pyro.param("tau_0").detach()tau1_optimal = pyro.param("tau_1").detach()kappa_optimal = pyro.param("kappa").detach()# We make a point-estimate of our latent variables using the posterior means of tau and kappa for the cluster params and weightsBayes_Rates = (tau0_optimal / tau1_optimal)Bayes_Weights = mix_weights(1. / (1. + kappa_optimal))def mixture_of_poisson(weights, rates, samples): return (weights * Poisson(rates).log_prob(samples.unsqueeze(-1)).exp()).sum(-1)likelihood = mixture_of_poisson(Bayes_Weights, Bayes_Rates, samples)plt.title("Number of Years vs. Sunspot Counts")plt.hist(data, bins=60, density=True, lw=0, alpha=0.75);plt.plot(samples, likelihood, label="Estimated Mixture Density")plt.legend()plt.show()

  The above plot is the mixture density of the Bayes estimators of the cluster parameters, weighted by their corresponding weights. As in the Gaussian example, we have taken the Bayes estimators of each cluster parameter and their corresponding weights by computing the posterior means of   lambda and   beta respectively.
 Below are plots of the behavior of the loss function (negative Trace_ELBO) over the SVI iterations during inference using Pyro, as well as a plot of the autocorrelations of the ELBO ‘time series’ versus iteration number. We can see that around 500 iterations, the loss stops decreasing significantly, so we can assume it takes around 500 iterations to achieve convergence. The autocorrelation plot reaches an autocorrelation very close to 0 around a lag of 500, further corroborating this hypothesis.Note that these are heuristics and do not necessarily imply convergence.
 elbo_plot = plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))elbo_ax = elbo_plot.add_subplot(1, 2, 1)elbo_ax.set_title("ELBO Value vs. Iteration Number for Pyro BBVI on Sunspot Data")elbo_ax.set_ylabel("ELBO")elbo_ax.set_xlabel("Iteration Number")elbo_ax.plot(np.arange(n_iter), losses)autocorr_ax = elbo_plot.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)autocorr_ax.acorr(np.asarray(losses), detrend=lambda x: x - x.mean(), maxlags=750, usevlines=False, marker=',')autocorr_ax.set_xlim(0, 500)autocorr_ax.axhline(0, ls="--", c="k", lw=1)autocorr_ax.set_title("Autocorrelation of ELBO vs. Lag for Pyro BBVI on Sunspot Data")autocorr_ax.set_xlabel("Lag")autocorr_ax.set_ylabel("Autocorrelation")elbo_plot.tight_layout()elbo_plot.show()
 Since we computed the approximate posterior of the DPMM that was fit to the long-term sunspot data, we can utilize some intrinsic metrics, such as the log predictive, posterior dispersion indices, and posterior predictive checks.
 Since the posterior predictive distribution for a Dirichlet process mixture model is itself a scale-mixture distribution that has an analytic approximation  (Blei and Jordan, 2004), this makes it particularly amenable to the aforementioned metrics:
  In particular, to compute the log predictive, we first compute the posterior predictive distribution (defined above) after performing variational inference on our model using a training subsample of our data. The log predictive is then the log value of the predictive density evaluated at each point in the test subsample:
 \[\log p(x_{new} | X) = \log \mathbb{E}_{\beta, \theta | X} \left[ p(x_{new} | \beta, \theta) \right]\]
 Since both the training samples and the testing samples were taken from the same dataset, we would expect the model to assign high probability to the test samples, despite not having seen them during inference. This gives a metric by which to select values of  \(T\),  \(\alpha\), and  \(G_0\), our hyperparameters: we would want to choose the values that maximize this value.
 We perform this process below with varying values of  \(\alpha\) to see what the optimal setting is.
